_authoring_notes: v3 first-pass rubric (no prior v2 on main). Authored via the scaleout author↔Gemini-critic loop under the strict-provenance bar: every scored goal must trace to a verbatim or paraphrased user message (instruction.md, user_simulation_prompt.md, or oracle_intents.json). Oracle-specific identifiers the user never voiced are stripped unless the user pinned them verbatim. Weights reflect user emphasis, not test_manifest.yaml F2P gate weights.

_provenance.goal_0: N/A — anti-gaming guard, derived from the harbor benchmark's defensive floor (no positive weight, can only zero the trial). Not a user ask.
_provenance.goal_1: Turn 0 ('there is a tool call ... that errors ... locate it, tell me why this happened') and Turn 11 ('ok, fix it, ensure lenght of id is within bounds!') are the central ask. The user-sim T2/T3/T4 turns establish the error is a foreign (github-copilot -> codex) tool id the codex backend rejects. Turn 10 ('what's the collision probability between shortHash?') shows the user cares that the derivation be collision-resistant, which is folded into this goal's collision-resistant clause. This is the primary behavioral deliverable: produce a codex-safe, bounded, clean item id for foreign tool calls. Behavioral, not pinned to any specific helper name or hashing algorithm.
_provenance.goal_2: The session's offending raw id is a compound 'call_...|<base64-with-slashes-and-plus>' string; the F2P 'call_id clean prefix (not mangled compound)' gate and the user's Turn 11 'ensure lenght of id is within bounds' instruction imply both halves of the identifier must be sanitized, not just the item half. Behavioral requirement on the call-id half, model-agnostic.
_provenance.goal_3: Turns T2/T3/T4 of the sim pin the bug specifically to the cross-provider replay (codex -> copilot -> codex), and the user insists the codex id 'is compatible with codex backend' — i.e. native ids should keep working. The p2p_tsgo / vitest regression gates and the golden patch's provider/api comparison guard against an over-broad fix. Guards against regressions for the non-foreign path.
_provenance.goal_4: Turn 12 ('test first with the string from copilot', corresponding to the sim's T13 'create or run a test file' trigger) is an explicit user ask: verify the fix with the actual copilot string before wrapping up. The golden patch adds exactly such a vitest test. Behavioral — any equivalent committed test driving the real input satisfies it.
_provenance.goal_5: Turn 15 ('Wrap it') instructs: 'Add or update the relevant package changelog entry under "## [Unreleased]" using the repo changelog rules.' The golden patch adds exactly such a changelog entry under '## [Unreleased]'. This is a persistent workspace end-state deliverable, not mere process. Kept behavioral — no exact wording or file path pinned beyond the package's unreleased changelog section.

_dropped_from_v2.note: No v2 rubric existed on main for this task. This is the first-pass rubric authored at the v3 bar via the scaleout author↔critic loop.

_added_for_coverage.note: Not applicable — no v2 baseline to diff against. Per-goal user-ask tracing lives in each goal's rationale and in _provenance.

_self_critique_log: goal_0: anti_gaming floor present, weight 0.0 — OK.
goal_1: weight=0.35, tier=core, user-turn cite=yes — provenance OK.
goal_2: weight=0.2, tier=important, user-turn cite=yes — provenance OK.
goal_3: weight=0.2, tier=important, user-turn cite=see rationale — provenance OK.
goal_4: weight=0.15, tier=important, user-turn cite=yes — provenance OK.
goal_5: weight=0.1, tier=important, user-turn cite=yes — provenance OK.
Gemini critic final: verdict=REVISE score=0.875 round=3.
strength: Excellent behavioral framing that correctly accommodates both the golden patch's 'hash item only' approach and the model patches' 'hash both' approach.
strength: Strong full-context coverage, capturing not just the core bug fix but also the requested regression test and changelog update.
strength: Thorough anti-cheating clauses in every goal that prevent hardcoding or trivial derivations.

residual risk: The citation mismatch in goal_4's rationale incorrectly attributes the 'test first' instruction to Turn 13 (which is actually a cleanup turn), potentially confusing the LLM judge about the user's actual sequence of requests.

_task_quality_flags.internet_with_live_origin: task.toml sets allow_internet=true. goal_0 anti-gaming guard names git-ops exfiltration as a zeroing trigger where applicable.

completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine code change that actually alters how foreign/cross-backend tool-call identifiers are produced during conversation replay, not a gaming artifact. Reject if the diff is empty/no-op, consists only of comments or whitespace, only reorders code without changing behavior, deletes or weakens the relevant tests instead of fixing the source, or hardcodes the one expected identifier string purely to satisfy a single test rather than computing it from the input. Also reject non-code artifacts such as a captured directory-tree banner (e.g. a diff whose only content is a '=== repo/ ===' header), git-ops exfiltration that fakes work instead of implementing it (e.g. 'git checkout main', 'git reset --hard origin/...'), and changes confined to temporary/unmerged scratch locations rather than the real source tree.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor (flagged, not scored). Calibrated against the reward-0.0 model patch, which led with a '=== pi-mono/ ===' captured-tree banner and altered the shared normalization in a way that did not produce the required clean identifier. The floor must catch generic stub/no-op/diff-pad/test-deletion cheats while staying behavioral.

completeness_goals.goal: After the fix, replaying a tool call that originated from a different backend than the target model converts the offending item identifier into a bounded, character-clean form that the target backend accepts. The resulting item identifier must keep the required leading 'fc_' prefix, contain only safe alphanumeric characters and underscores (no slashes, plus signs, equals, pipes, or other punctuation carried over from the original backend's raw id), and never exceed the backend's 64-character length limit. The replacement identifier must be derived from the offending input in a collision-resistant manner (e.g. a hash of the original id) rather than hardcoded or trivially derived (such as from the string's length or a constant counter), so that distinct original ids do not routinely map to the same replacement. This must hold for the real offending identifier from the session, and must not be merely asserted by a comment or a loosened test.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 ('there is a tool call ... that errors ... locate it, tell me why this happened') and Turn 11 ('ok, fix it, ensure lenght of id is within bounds!') are the central ask. The user-sim T2/T3/T4 turns establish the error is a foreign (github-copilot -> codex) tool id the codex backend rejects. Turn 10 ('what's the collision probability between shortHash?') shows the user cares that the derivation be collision-resistant, which is folded into this goal's collision-resistant clause. This is the primary behavioral deliverable: produce a codex-safe, bounded, clean item id for foreign tool calls. Behavioral, not pinned to any specific helper name or hashing algorithm.

completeness_goals.goal: The companion call identifier of the same foreign tool call also comes out clean and well-formed: it keeps the required leading 'call_' prefix and contains only safe alphanumeric characters and underscores, with none of the raw backend punctuation (slash, plus, equals, pipe) leaking through into the final identifier. The call identifier must be derived from the actual input in a collision-resistant manner (e.g. a hash of the original call id, or preserving an already-clean original call id), not hardcoded to a constant or trivially derived (such as from string length) purely to pass this goal. A solution that fixes the item id but leaves a mangled or compound call id does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: The session's offending raw id is a compound 'call_...|<base64-with-slashes-and-plus>' string; the F2P 'call_id clean prefix (not mangled compound)' gate and the user's Turn 11 'ensure lenght of id is within bounds' instruction imply both halves of the identifier must be sanitized, not just the item half. Behavioral requirement on the call-id half, model-agnostic.

completeness_goals.goal: The fix is scoped to foreign/cross-backend tool calls and does not corrupt ordinary same-backend tool-call identifiers. Tool calls whose originating backend matches the target model must continue to round-trip with valid identifiers (correct prefixes, no broken/empty ids); the foreign-id transformation must be an additive special case for the cross-backend situation, not a blanket rewrite that mangles or drops well-formed native ids. Achieving the foreign-id fix by indiscriminately hashing or truncating every identifier such that legitimate same-backend calls break does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turns T2/T3/T4 of the sim pin the bug specifically to the cross-provider replay (codex -> copilot -> codex), and the user insists the codex id 'is compatible with codex backend' — i.e. native ids should keep working. The p2p_tsgo / vitest regression gates and the golden patch's provider/api comparison guard against an over-broad fix. Guards against regressions for the non-foreign path.

completeness_goals.goal: The change is accompanied by a deterministic, committed regression test that drives the real foreign tool-call identifier from the session through the conversion path and asserts the resulting identifier is clean, correctly prefixed, and within the length bound. The test must exercise the actual production conversion code (not re-implement or stub it) and must use the genuine offending input string, so that it would fail against the un-fixed code. A test that hardcodes the expected output without invoking the real code path, or that is deleted/loosened to pass trivially, does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 12 ('test first with the string from copilot', corresponding to the sim's T13 'create or run a test file' trigger) is an explicit user ask: verify the fix with the actual copilot string before wrapping up. The golden patch adds exactly such a vitest test. Behavioral — any equivalent committed test driving the real input satisfies it.

completeness_goals.goal: The relevant package's changelog is updated to document this fix as part of wrapping up the work. A new entry describing the foreign/cross-backend tool-call identifier fix is added under the unreleased section of the changelog for the package that was changed. The entry must describe the actual fix that was made (not a placeholder, an empty heading, or boilerplate copied without substance), and must live in the package's real changelog file rather than a scratch or temporary location.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 15 ('Wrap it') instructs: 'Add or update the relevant package changelog entry under "## [Unreleased]" using the repo changelog rules.' The golden patch adds exactly such a changelog entry under '## [Unreleased]'. This is a persistent workspace end-state deliverable, not mere process. Kept behavioral — no exact wording or file path pinned beyond the package's unreleased changelog section.

decomposition_notes: Evidence tier is rich: a hand-curated golden patch plus three model patches (two reward-1.0, one reward-0.0). The behavioral core, recoverable from instruction + user-sim trigger table + F2P tests, is: a Copilot-originated tool call replayed against the OpenAI Codex backend carries an item id ('fc_I9b95oN1wD_...') that codex rejects because foreign backend ids are character-normalized rather than re-derived into a bounded codex-safe shape. goal_1 (clean, bounded, correctly-prefixed item id for foreign tool calls, weight 0.45) is the dominant ask, tying directly to Turn 0 ('tell me why this happened') and Turn 11 ('fix it, ensure lenght of id is within bounds'). goal_2 (clean call-id half, 0.20) mirrors the second F2P gate and the user's both-halves length concern. goal_3 (don't regress same-backend native ids, 0.20) encodes the user's T4 insistence that the codex id is natively compatible plus the tsgo/vitest regression gates, guarding against an over-broad hash-everything fix — and is why the reward-0.0 patch's shared-normalization change is suspect. goal_4 (committed regression test with the real copilot string, 0.15) captures the explicit Turn 12 'test first with the string from copilot' ask. goal_5 (changelog entry under the unreleased section, 0.10) captures the Turn-15 'Wrap it' instruction to add/update the relevant package changelog under '## [Unreleased]', which the golden patch includes as a persistent end-state change. The reward-1.0 model patches hash BOTH call and item halves while preserving the same-backend path, and the F2P call-id regex accepts that, so goals are kept behavioral (no helper/function/variable names, no specific hashing scheme) to admit either the golden 'hash item only' or the winning 'hash both' implementation. Pure-diagnosis turns (T2-T4 'why would this happen') and the ad-hoc repro-script turns (T4/T5) are investigation, not kept end-state deliverables — the standalone repro scripts the golden patch left at the repo root ('pi-codex-repro.ts' etc.) are scratch artifacts not required of a correct solution, so they are not scored. The cleanup turns (T13/T14 'remove heap snapshots / mbtree file') target profiling artifacts the original user had generated locally during the session; those files are not present in a fresh eval workspace, so a 'workspace contains no heap snapshots' goal would be trivially-satisfied or unverifiable and is therefore not scored — but the Turn-15 changelog deliverable, which IS reliably present in the golden patch and persists in the source tree, is scored via goal_5. The commit/push/GitHub-comment items in the Turn-15 stanza are process actions (git plumbing, no GitHub issue in context) with no checkable workspace end-state, so they remain unscored. Weights deliberately differ from the F2P manifest (which splits 0.50/0.50 between item-id and call-id gates); user-importance front-loads the item-id behavior and adds the non-regression, test, and changelog asks the manifest's two gates do not weight. Sum of weighted goals = 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00.
